IsA: It is a two-dimensional piece of art.
It is a dedicated suffrage memorial.
It is a historic heritage place.
HasFeatureOf: It is characterized by colourful tiles.
It is characterized by Kate Sheppard's scroll.
It is characterized by illustrations of iconic motifs of Auckland.
It is characterized by the Southern Cross.
It is characterized by depictions of eleven women significant to the suffrage movement.
UsedFor: It is used for commemorating the centenary of women securing the vote in New Zealand.
It is used for promoting public education on the suffrage.
It is used for signifying the women's suffrage petition.
It is used for symbolizing a weaving together of cultures.
It is used for signifying transformation and growth.
LocatedIn: It is located at Khartoum Place, Auckland CBD.
It is located on the stairs that connect Kitchener and Lorne Street.
It is located leading to Auckland Art Gallery.
It is located in Lower Khartoum Place.
BuiltBy: It was built by Jan Morrison.
It was built by Claudia Pond Eyley.
OriginatedIn: It originated in the idea to commemorate the centenary of women's suffrage.
It originated in a proposal by artist Jan Morrison.
CreatedBy: It was created by artists Jan Morrison and Claudia Pond Eyley.
It was created with the help of fellow feminist artist Claudia Pond Eyley.
It was created with a specific focus on the women of northern New Zealand.
ProducedBy: It was produced by a community-led project.
It was produced with funding from the Suffrage Centennial Trust / Whakatū Wahine.
It was produced with funding from the Auckland City Council.
CausedBy: It is caused by the centenary of women securing the vote in New Zealand.
It is caused by the desire to commemorate the suffrage movement.
Affects: It affects the view to the Auckland Art Gallery.
It affects the space at Khartoum Place.
ConsistsOf: It consists of 12 panels made up of 2,000 colourful tiles.
It consists of a water feature.
It consists of representations of Māori kete.
It consists of representations of koru.
It consists of illustrations of iconic motifs of Auckland.
It consists of depictions of eleven women significant to the suffrage movement.
PartOf: It is part of the Khartoum Place upgrade.
It is part of the unitary plan process.
It is part of the Auckland Council's unitary plan.
It is part of the Heritage New Zealand listing.
BelongsTo: It belongs to the heritage of New Zealand.
It belongs to the history of women's suffrage in New Zealand.
KnownFor: It is known for being one of the first and few dedicated suffrage memorials in New Zealand.
It is known for its depiction of significant women in the suffrage movement.
It is known for its symbolism of cultural weaving and transformation.
It is known for its heritage listing as a category 1 historic place.
AssociatedWith: It is associated with the centenary of women securing the vote in New Zealand.
It is associated with the Suffrage Centennial Trust / Whakatū Wahine.
It is associated with the women's suffrage movement in New Zealand.
It is associated with the dignity of women.
NamedAfter: It is named after women's suffrage.
It is named after the suffrage movement.
It is named after Te Hā o Hine Suffrage Place.
HeldIn: It was held in Khartoum Place.
About: It is about the centenary of women securing the vote in New Zealand.
It is about the suffrage movement in New Zealand.
It is about the women of northern New Zealand who contributed to the movement.
It is about the historical importance of the suffrage movement.
It is about the dignity of women.
InfluencedBy: It was influenced by the Suffrage Centennial Trust / Whakatū Wahine.
It was influenced by the Auckland City Council.
It was influenced by the feminist art movement.
It was influenced by the Māori proverb Me aro koe ki te hā o Hine ahu one.
Influenced: It influenced the decision to protect it as a heritage item.
It influenced the renaming of Lower Khartoum Place to Te Hā o Hine Suffrage Place.
